meta.title: Unit 2 — MS Word for Legal Documentation
meta.subtitle: Computer Information · Law students
meta.author: Academic Content Factory

slides.title: Unit 2
slides.subtitle: MS Word for Legal Documentation
slides.notes: Teach Unit 2 as a modular block (6 hours).

slides.title: Unit 2 — MS Word for Legal Documentation
slides.notes: Six hours, 10% weight. Live-demo drafting. Insist on styles over manual formatting. Track changes models senior review workflow.

slides.title: Word for Professional Legal Documents
bullets: Primary tool for notices, agreements, petitions, affidavits
Professional output needs styles, not random font clicks
Consistent layout signals credibility to courts
Revision tools support supervised collaboration
Export to PDF after final proofreading
Syllabus practicals include notice and affidavit drafts
slides.notes: Set expectations: formatting discipline equals professional credibility. Students will submit tracked-revision samples in lab work.

slides.title: Creating and Formatting Documents
bullets: Set font, size, line spacing, and alignment first
Use Styles (Heading 1, Body Text) for consistency
Styles enable automatic Table of Contents
Avoid multiple Enter presses for vertical spacing
Times New Roman or Arial per college/court norms
Save versions with clear filenames
slides.notes: Demonstrate applying a Style versus manual bold/size. Styles are the professional standard and save time on long petitions.

slides.title: Page Layout, Headers, and Footers
bullets: Margins, orientation, and paper size affect filing readability
Headers: matter title, confidentiality label, court name
Footers: page numbers essential on long petitions
Section breaks when header/footer content changes
Columns rarely used; tables preferred for structured data
Preview print layout before submission
slides.notes: Common pitfall: forgetting page numbers. Show header/footer insertion and when to use section breaks in multi-part documents.

slides.title: Tables in Legal Documents
bullets: Organize parties, timelines, property schedules, fee lists
Insert via Insert → Table; define columns before data
Use table styles for readable borders and shading
Avoid merged cells that break later sorting
Keep tables simple for court and client clarity
Convert tabular facts from narrative where helpful
slides.notes: Tables beat space-bar alignment. Show a party-details table and a limitation timeline as legal examples.

slides.title: Mail Merge — Steps and Legal Uses
bullets: Step 1: Create template with merge fields (Name, Address)
Step 2: Connect data source (Excel or CSV list)
Step 3: Insert fields and preview each record
Step 4: Complete merge — print, PDF, or individual files
Use for batch client letters and workshop invitations
High-yield procedural question in SEE and CIA
slides.notes: Mail merge steps are exam favorites. Demo with a small client list producing personalized notices or update letters.

slides.title: References, Citations, and Footnotes
bullets: References tab manages footnotes and endnotes
Footnotes for academic seminar and project work
Bibliography tools support consistent citation lists
Follow college citation style when specified
Cross-references link to sections, figures, or tables
Professional filings may use manual citation per court rules
slides.notes: Distinguish academic footnote workflow from court filing conventions. Students need both awareness for seminars and practice drafts.

slides.title: Track Changes and Comments
bullets: Revision mode records insertions, deletions, and formatting
Comments flag questions without altering text
Supervisor reviews; drafter accepts or rejects edits
Essential in chambers and moot coach feedback
Deliver clean copy unless markup is requested
Never accept all changes without reading each one
slides.notes: Workflow: draft → review → accept/reject → finalize. Pitfall: submitting a document with visible markup unintentionally.

slides.title: Legal Drafting Document Types

left.heading: Notices & Agreements
bullets: Notice: parties, facts, demand, timeline, signature
Agreement: title, parties, recitals, clauses, signatures
Clear numbered paragraphs for facts
Demand or obligation stated in plain language

right.heading: Petitions & Affidavits
bullets: Petition: court title, parties, facts, grounds, prayer
Affidavit: deponent details, numbered facts, verification
Verification or oath block at the end
Structure matters as much as formatting

slides.notes: Students must outline skeleton structures for exams. Formatting supports substance—learn both. Lab: draft a practice notice and affidavit.

slides.title: Unit 2 — Exam Tips
bullets: Memorize four mail merge steps in order
Explain purpose of track changes in legal drafting
Outline structure of notice vs affidavit vs petition
Name five syllabus Word features with one-line purpose
Procedure questions: headers, footers, page numbers
Pitfall: spaces instead of styles or proper tables
slides.notes: Revise mail merge and track changes first—they appear repeatedly. Practice outlining document skeletons from memory.

slides.title: Unit 2 — Next steps
bullets: Complete unit labs
Attempt unit Q&A bank
Submit unit assignment
Revise using revision sheet
slides.notes: Point students to materials/labs and materials/qa for this unit.
